segments.text: So, today we begin our lecture 12, so we begin with lecture 12, let us recall the things
that we did last time, we talked about the concept of vector space, then we talked about
the concept of a subspace, so just as a reminder, vector space was basically a non-empty
set which had two operations, one about addition, one about multiplication by a scalar
and we said it is closed in the addition and multiplication by scalar and it had total ten
axioms that we talked about, various laws like distributive law, commutative law, existence of
zero, existence of inverses, so all those things we had talked about and subspace was basically
a subset of a vector space which is a vector space in its own right, so this is like so-called,
I would say crude way of putting things, but since it is a reminder, so I am just giving the
essence of it, subspace of a vector space is a non-empty subset of a vector space which is
a vector space in its own right and we had seen how to check something is a vector space,
how to check something is a subspace, so let me recall for subspace, all we needed to ensure
was that if I say a subset S of my vector space V is a subspace, then it should be non-empty
and it should be closed under and the third thing we want is it should be closed under
scalar, now these two things can be combined under one simple idea and we can say that S is closed
under linear combination, so we pick two vectors in here and form every possible linear combination,
all the combinations thus formed would be inside set S, so what this means, so what I will do
I will try to give you the meaning of it, so if I pick two vectors, let us say U and V which
belong to my set S and suppose C1 and C2 are linear numbers, in general you can call them
as scalars depending on the field we are using, but since our attention goes to the
numbers, so this is a subspace if we can guarantee that C1 U plus C2 V can always be inside S,
so if it is closed under linear combinations, then we can say that our set S is a subspace
of our vector space, so this is what I am just trying to recall in a nutshell of things
that we saw, now what we will do is I come up with some examples of how addition and
multiplication can be defined on different sets, something which is less familiar to us and we
check how well whether they will form a vector space or not, so let me construct some simple
examples first that can help us understand or sort of come out of our comfort zone,
so here is one example, so here is my space, here is my set, this must be a very
lesser to us, basically I am saying let us again look at the two dimensional vectors in real space,
however this time I am going to define my addition and subtraction slightly differently,
so this time I am going to say that suppose you want to add two vectors x y and say a b,
this is how you are going to add x plus a y plus b, so this is my definition of addition
which is the same as what we have seen so many times, here is my definition of multiplication
by a scalar, notice that I want to define it like this, I am only multiplying the
second component not the first one, suppose this is my new definition of addition and
multiplication by a scalar, the question we are asking is, is V a vector space under
these operations, so what it means to say is that we are taking a familiar set namely R2,
but this time we are trying to come up with a different way of adding things and multiplying
the scalars, so that in some sense it will lead us to a different kind of geometry,
now under this new set of operations where it is still be a vector space or this one of
the axioms or some of the axioms go wrong, so what we will do is we go through all the
axioms one by one and see all of them are followed over, is there a problem somewhere,
so let us try to analyze is this a vector space, so first of all we will talk about closure,
so let us talk about the closure part, is closure under addition, is closure under addition
there or not, as you can see it is the usual addition the way we add things, so if I
take a vector in V, I pick another vector in V, as you can see the only criteria for something
being in V is that both of these are real numbers, so I would say closure under addition,
it is the usual addition I am guaranteed the closure, so that is something which is not a,
which we can check fairly easily, now we have to worry about closure under multiplication,
is it closed under multiplication, well let us see if I pick a vector in R2 in V and multiplied
by scalar under this definition, the first component and the second component both belong
to R, so suppose I pick x, y in V and suppose I have a scalar C, then C times x, y which is
basically x, C, y both the components, so both x and C, y are in R, this implies this vector x,
C, y will also belong to V, hence it is closed under multiplication also, so closure is not a
problem is closed under both these operations, so maybe it is a vector space, but remember
we have to talk about other axioms, so what I will do is I will pick one or two axioms as
an example just to illustrate the process and then we will see if we are able to randomly come
up with an idea of which axiom may fail in this case, so let us try the axiom about the zero
vector, is there a zero vector, well I claim that 0, 0 which is a member of V is the
vector, now the easiest way to check is let us perform the addition on defined on V,
based on the definition of addition we are going to get this, so existence of zero vector is
guaranteed to us and hence at least this axiom about the existence of a zero vector for an
identity is also taken care of, so we have a zero vector, what about commutativity,
is the addition commutative that means if I switch the order of these guys will I still get the

will it equal the right hand side, so let us try to understand what the right hand side will

obtained here, is this right hand side equal to the left hand side, as you add the way

definitely these two things need not be equal for all the use of x, so that means this axiom
will fail in this particular given set, hence so what we have obtained is that C plus D

Now, I give you a definition to add this, here is how we will add, so x you will add into y is x times y,

in fact until we have the zero element we could not even have talked about the inverses

if you look at these operations you might get reminded of something, if you look at this addition

and I define two sets of addition and multiplication and somehow it turns out
to be a vector space under both those operations, will the two vector spaces
be the same in the sense isomorphic or will it turn out to be different,

and I define an operation multiplication with the same set V I define another operation of

Okay, let us come to further understanding the subspace structure.
So, let us come back to things, I had made a comment in the previous lecture that all the
spaces of R2, R1 was R2 itself, the other was just having the zero vector and the third thing
was all straight lines, I had made a kind of a statement which I never proved,
I said these are the only subspaces of R2 and there is no other question is why are these

position to answer it. Now, you will have to recall I had already shown you that
the set containing just the zero vector is always a subspace, of course the entire space
has to be a subspace by default and I had also shown you that all the straight lines
had proven that all the straight lines that pass through the origin will be a subspace,

subspaces, so I will try to give you a idea of the proof, proof is not very involved,
but here is a basic idea that can help us. Now, suppose I have a subspace of R2, if S
has only the zero vector then nothing to prove, because we have already shown
that the set containing just a zero vector is always a subspace.
So, now assume that S has some non-zero vector, so that U belong to S such that
U is not a zero vector, so that means S has a non-zero vector, now since S is a subspace
this implies that it must be closed under multiplication, so that means C times U must

if I have given you that my subspace contains a non-zero vector, let us say that non-zero

all multiples of U, so that means it contains a straight line that passes through the origin,
so it contains the straight line that passes through zero, zero. Now, if S does not have

S is basically a straight line, so that we already know all the straight lines through origin

time passes through the origin and is in the direction of the vector U, that means V lies

so imagine what will happen if I have all possible combinations of U and V, this will

If I call this coefficient matrix as A, the coefficient matrix is invertible and from the
